artifacts.content:
Hi Ana,

Finally catching my breath after a long day and wanted to actually write instead of just sending you random memes.

Work has been…a lot. Being Deputy Director since 2021 means I’m the one who has to sign off when staff cross the line with racially biased practice. In June 2022 I had to approve a written reprimand and mandatory bias training for a Senior CSW who made discriminatory comments about a Black dad during a Team Decision Making meeting at the Riverview Family Resource Center. It was awful – I kept thinking about Papá at that Oakview Community Center town hall back on 9/9/19, talking about how humiliating it was when APS showed up after his congestive heart failure hospitalization. This is exactly the kind of harm I’m trying to prevent, and yet here we are.

On top of that, I’m still co‑chairing the REI Steering Committee and doing those closed‑door listening sessions. In April 2022, in Conference Room B at headquarters, two of my own staff (including Jasmine from my unit) disclosed they’d filed EEO complaints about racially disparate caseloads and retaliation by Karen Liu. Remember, she’s the same manager who wrote me up over that Chronicle case in 2016 when I ended up on 6‑week unpaid leave and almost quit. Full circle, in the worst way.

Meanwhile, normal life keeps going. Bella has soccer practice tomorrow at 4:30, Mateo’s trumpet rehearsal is at 6, and I promised Daniel I’d actually cook instead of grabbing pupusas again. I’m thinking lentil soup so I can stretch it for lunches.

How are things at Stockton Family Health Clinic? Next time you’re down for the weekend, let’s set aside a night just for us, no kids, no work talk (or at least less work talk).

Te quiero,
Moni

Personal reflection – late night journaling

I’ve technically been with Riverview County DCFS since June 2008 – 17+ years now – but the path has never been straight.

I still think about August–September 2016, when Karen Liu issued that formal reprimand over the high‑profile removal that landed in Sacramento Superior Court and on the front page of the Riverview Chronicle. Six‑week unpaid leave, sitting at the kitchen table with Daniel, writing pros/cons lists about resigning. I remember emailing him after that meeting, saying I didn’t know if I could stay at the county after eight years. Somehow I came back, and now I’m Deputy Director.

Funny to think that for the first 1.5 years (June 2008–Dec 2009) I was only a “limited‑term” hire because of that I‑9 audit. HR – Denise Porter – going back and forth with the USCIS Sacramento Field Office about my SSN under Monica Leticia Martinez vs. Reyes. No benefits, retirement contributions on hold. It’s all so mundane and yet it shaped our finances.

And then February 2012, payroll randomly coding me as “terminated” for three weeks after my name change, cutting off our insurance right when Bella had that asthma visit at Riverview Pediatric Clinic. I was furious – almost four years of continuous service and still treated like I was disposable.

Layer on March 2013, when Papá had the major heart attack at Mercy General and I used the full 12 weeks of FMLA while trying to be a brand‑new supervisor. I really did almost walk away then.

Seventeen years sounds solid on paper. In my body, it feels like a series of near‑exits I somehow talked myself out of.

Event: Closed session briefing – Board of Supervisors (REI disproportionality data)
Date: Thursday, March 9, 2023
Time: 3:00–5:00 PM
Location: Riverview County Administration Building – 5th Floor, Board Chambers (Closed Session)
Attendees: Board of Supervisors; County Counsel; CWS Executive Team

Notes:
- Present updated data on hotline referrals, substantiations, and removals disaggregated by race/ethnicity and supervisorial district.
- Highlight that Latino families in District 3 (Danny’s district) are being reported to the hotline at approximately 2.5x the rate of White families.
- Be explicit about potential links between truancy enforcement at Riverview High School and increased mandated reports from school personnel.
- Acknowledge conflict‑of‑interest dynamics: Daniel Reyes as both my husband and a Supervisor whose policies may be contributing to disparities. Maintain professional boundaries; no side conversations about data at home beyond what’s public record.
- Anticipate pushback from at least one Board member about “over‑reporting vs. real risk.” Prepare examples from Oakview Community Center town hall (9/9/19) where Papá and other caregivers described hotline calls that felt like racial profiling.
- Be ready for questions about staff accountability: reference June 2022 corrective action plan for Senior CSW who made discriminatory comments toward a Black father during a TDM at the Riverview Family Resource Center (without naming worker).
- Self‑care: block 30 minutes after meeting to walk around the river trail before driving home. No checking work email after 9 PM.

metadata.title: Closed session – REI data + Danny’s district

artifacts.content:
Hi Carla,

I’ve been thinking about your message and wanted to respond more fully than a quick Teams chat.

Since September 2021, in this REI executive sponsor role, I’ve been quietly coaching Elena Torres in HR on how to document and address repeated racially insensitive comments from Bob Hastings. You probably heard about the November 3, 2021 leadership REI training at the County Administration Building where he asked, in front of about 25 staff, whether “our Black parents just don’t value education.” That session still sits in my stomach.

It reminded me of October 2014, when I was supervising the mixed ER/FM unit and two of my Black social workers came into my office at the Riverview main building in tears because a manager had accused them of “playing the race card” in supervision. I stayed almost two hours off the clock helping them process and draft something for EEO. Back then I was the unofficial “safe person”; now it’s become part of my formal project role.

I also keep thinking about you in May 2020, going through that written warning over the hotline call where they said you profiled a Black mom, and then committing to six months of racial equity coaching at the main office. That’s the kind of accountability with support I want us to normalize, instead of defensiveness or silent tolerance of comments like Bob’s.

Day‑to‑day life is still chaos: Bella’s JV soccer schedule just dropped (games almost every Tuesday), Mateo’s band concert is next month, and Papá’s cardiology follow‑up at Mercy General is on my calendar again. But this culture work is the through‑line.

If you’re open to it, I’d love to have you Zoom into one of our newer coaching circles to share what actually helped you shift your practice.

Con cariño,
Monica

metadata.subject: REI work and what accountability can look like

artifacts.content:
Case/role boundary reflection – PPE & kinship decisions

I keep circling back to how blurry my roles got between 2016 and 2020 when I was Program Manager for Permanency and Placement.

One example: 2018, Relative/NREFM Placement unit. I overruled a supervisor to keep three siblings with their undocumented maternal aunt, Carmen Alvarez, in Sacramento instead of moving them to a White foster family in Riverview. During a home visit she confided she was terrified a formal appeal might expose her immigration status. I documented the clinical reasons – attachment, cultural continuity – but I still worry about how much I relied on information she shared off the record.

Then May 2019, when the Resource Family Approval unit denied my own parents, Jorge and Rosa, as backup placement for my cousin’s kids in Oakview. They flagged Papá’s chronic heart condition and that 1998 DUI in Sacramento as disqualifying risk factors. I used my Program Manager authority to override the denial with conditions. I wrote an ethical reflection at the time about conflict of interest, but I don’t know if that’s enough.

And outside the office, between October 2020 and May 2021, as co‑chair of the Permanency and Placement Equity Workgroup, I started paying for Uber rides on my personal credit card – about $65 per meeting – to get Maria Gutierrez from the Riverbend Apartments to our evening meetings at the Riverview County Community Resource Center. She’d told me on 10/22/20 that her license was suspended for unpaid tickets and a prior DUI. I wanted to remove barriers for a Latina parent rep, but I also blurred personal/professional lines.

Need to build clearer guidance for managers doing REI work so we’re not improvising ethics case by case.

Hi Dr. Alvarez,

I hope you’re well. I’ve been thinking about our last conversation on how “project roles” in equity work can slide into direct case intervention, and I wanted to share a concrete example that’s been on my mind.

Between January and June 2018, when I was co‑chairing the Permanency and Placement Equity Workgroup, I intervened in at least three specific placement decisions. One that still haunts me is D.J., a 9‑year‑old Black boy in the Oakview neighborhood. During a February 14, 2018 home visit, his aunt told me she was off her depression meds and had missed two of his psych med checks at Riverview Children’s Behavioral Health Clinic. A supervisor had already recommended moving him to a level 12 group home in Sacramento. I overrode that recommendation and pushed for in‑home supports instead. It was the right call clinically and through an REI lens, but I was acting more like a case manager than a project lead.

Around that same time (July 2019–March 2021), I became the de facto liaison with Deputy County Counsel Mark Ellison on high‑profile dependency cases involving Black and Native families. After an October 2020 removal decision that was appealed for alleged racial bias in our SDM assessment, he started asking me to personally review and sign off on petitions before filing at the Riverview Juvenile Courthouse. My REI work was suddenly intertwined with legal risk management.

Add in the informal support roles – arranging transportation for parent reps, late‑night calls with youth – and it feels like there’s no clean boundary between “equity initiative” and operational decision‑making.

If you have any articles or frameworks on ethical boundaries for leaders doing anti‑racism work inside systems, I’d love recommendations. I’m trying to mentor Angela Chen and the newer managers so they don’t repeat my mistakes.

Warmly,
Monica

metadata.subject: When REI project work becomes casework

artifacts.content:
Daniel: You home yet?

Monica: Just left Oakview. Stopped by Mami & Papá’s to drop groceries and set up his meds for the week.

Daniel: How’s he doing?

Monica: Tired. We talked a bit about that Oakview Community Center town hall back on 9/9/19, when he spoke up about the elder abuse report after his CHF hospitalization. He still gets angry remembering the hotline call.

Daniel: I don’t blame him.

Monica: Same. It’s in my head a lot as I prep for the next “Courageous Conversations on Race” circle. Remember when you co‑facilitated those staff circles in the DCFS training room between March 2018 and Nov 2019? I’m thinking of inviting a few newer workers over for a debrief dinner here at 1842 Willow Crest Ct after the next child fatality review. Like the ones we used to host – you, me, a pot of pozole, staff of color needing to exhale.

Daniel: I’m in, as long as it’s not the same night as Bella’s game.

Monica: Already checked – Mateo has soccer at Riverbend Community Park Saturday morning but nothing that night. I’ll put it on the calendar.

Daniel: Ok. Also, did you ever talk to Ana about that June 12, 2021 listening session at St. Mary’s Parish Hall? She mentioned CPS coming out over my nephew’s asthma meds.

Monica: Yeah, I did. She’s still pretty raw about the neglect investigation, especially because she works in the pediatric asthma program at Stockton Family Health Clinic. We owe her a weekend here with zero judgment and lots of pan dulce.

Daniel: Agreed.

Monica: Pulling into the driveway. Save me some café de olla.

Notes to self:

- The California Disproportionality Project Cdp: true
- Capp: false

- A2 Were You With The Agency When Or If They Participated In Any Of Those Anti Racism Initiatives Listed Above No: false
